title: Natus NicoletOne EEG System - No Signal or Poor Electrode Signal Quality
description: Troubleshooting loss of EEG signal or poor waveform quality caused by electrodes, impedance, or connection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while electrodes are connected to an active patient study unless clinically appropriate. Pause or stop the recording if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Signal Issue
steps.instructions: Observe the EEG display for flat lines, excessive noise, or intermittent signals to confirm the issue is reproducible.

steps.title: Check Electrode Placement and Skin Preparation
steps.instructions: Ensure proper electrode placement, verify skin preparation, and confirm conductive paste or gel is applied.

steps.title: Check Electrode Impedance Values
steps.instructions: Use impedance check function and ensure values are within acceptable range; re-prep electrodes if needed.

steps.title: Inspect Lead Wires and Headbox Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure all electrodes and cables are securely connected and inspect for damage.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Electrical Interference
steps.instructions: Move cables away from power sources and eliminate nearby interference sources.

steps.title: Verify Amplifier/Headbox Detection
steps.instructions: Confirm hardware is recognized and properly connected to the system.

steps.title: Perform a System Restart
steps.instructions: Restart software, amplifier, and computer to clear temporary faults.

steps.title: Swap Components to Isolate the Issue
steps.instructions: Test known-good electrodes, cables, or channels to identify faulty components.

CCR.Complaint: EEG system showing no signal on multiple channels during patient setup.
CCR.Cause: Poor electrode contact and high impedance due to inadequate skin preparation.
CCR.Resolution: Re-prepped skin, reapplied electrodes, and verified impedance within acceptable range; normal signal restored.

helpfulDetails: Final system status (working or removed from service)

title: Natus NicoletOne EEG System - Amplifier Not Detected or Hardware Communication Error
description: Troubleshooting amplifier detection failures and communication errors preventing EEG signal acquisition.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the system is actively being used for patient monitoring or diagnostics, stop the study and transition to another working EEG system.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact error message on the acquisition software and observe for amplifier not detected message, no EEG waveforms, or inability to start acquisition.

steps.title: Check Amplifier Power Status (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Verify amplifier power indicator is on, ensure power cable is connected, and confirm battery is charged if applicable.

steps.title: Inspect Data/Interface Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Reseat all interface cables, reconnect firmly, and inspect for bent pins or damage.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Cable or Port
steps.instructions: Swap cables and test alternate ports to rule out cable or port failure.

steps.title: Restart the System in Proper Sequence
steps.instructions: Power off software, turn off amplifier, restart computer, then power on amplifier before launching software.

steps.title: Check Software Configuration Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct amplifier model is selected and no incorrect device profile is loaded.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Check for damaged connectors, liquid exposure, overheating, or unusual odor.

CCR.Complaint: EEG system reported amplifier not detected and unable to start acquisition.
CCR.Cause: Faulty amplifier interface cable preventing communication with acquisition system.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced cable and verified amplifier detection and normal EEG signal acquisition.

helpfulDetails: Any visible damage or environmental concerns
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Natus NicoletOne EEG System - Software Crash or Acquisition Failure
description: Software freezing, crashes, or inability to start or continue EEG acquisition due to external connections, system resources, or configuration issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop the study and safely disconnect the patient from the system if acquisition is unstable or frozen. Move to another EEG system if monitoring is required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to reproduce the problem. Observe if the software freezes, closes, or displays an error, and determine if acquisition fails to start or stops unexpectedly.

steps.title: Check System Power and Hardware Connections
steps.instructions: Verify amplifier power, confirm USB or Ethernet connections, and inspect for loose or damaged cables.

steps.title: Confirm Amplifier Detection in Software
steps.instructions: Check that the amplifier is detected in the NicoletOne software and restart acquisition services if needed.

steps.title: Restart Software Application
steps.instructions: Close and relaunch the EEG software, ensuring no background processes are still running.

steps.title: Reboot the Workstation
steps.instructions: Perform a full system reboot to clear memory and resolve OS-level issues.

steps.title: Check System Resources and Background Processes
steps.instructions: Close unnecessary applications, monitor CPU and memory usage, and verify sufficient storage space.

steps.title: Verify Network and Storage Paths
steps.instructions: Confirm network connectivity and ensure storage locations or mapped drives are accessible.

steps.title: Test with a New Study or Patient Profile
steps.instructions: Create a new test recording to rule out corrupted study files or configuration issues.

steps.title: Check Peripheral Inputs and Accessories
steps.instructions: Inspect electrodes, headbox connections, and accessories, and remove unnecessary peripherals during testing.

CCR.Complaint: EEG system software crashes during acquisition and fails to record data.
CCR.Cause: Unstable communication between amplifier and workstation due to intermittent USB connection.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated and replaced USB cable; verified stable acquisition and normal operation.

title: Natus NicoletOne EEG System - Network or Data Storage / Export Failure
description: Troubleshooting failed data saves, export errors, or network connectivity issues preventing EEG study storage, transfer, or retrieval.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot during an active EEG study if it risks data loss or interruption. Ensure patient monitoring is stable or transition to a backup system if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm if the study is failing to save locally, if exports are failing, and identify any error messages to determine where the failure occurs.

steps.title: Check Network Connectivity (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Verify Ethernet connection, check network lights, confirm valid IP address, and test access to network locations.

steps.title: Verify Network Path or Server Availability
steps.instructions: Check mapped drives, confirm server access from another workstation, and verify credentials and permissions.

steps.title: Check Local Storage Capacity
steps.instructions: Ensure sufficient hard drive space, review stored studies, and archive or delete data per policy if needed.

steps.title: Test Export to Alternate Media
steps.instructions: Attempt export to USB and alternate network locations to isolate the issue source.

steps.title: Check Software Status and Restart Application
steps.instructions: Restart the NicoletOne software and retry saving or exporting.

steps.title: Perform a Full System Reboot
steps.instructions: Restart the system to resolve OS-level communication or service issues.

steps.title: Check User Permissions and Login Credentials
steps.instructions: Verify user permissions and re-enter credentials to ensure authorized access to storage locations.

CCR.Complaint: EEG system unable to export studies to network drive; export failed repeatedly.
CCR.Cause: Network connection lost due to disconnected Ethernet cable.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected network cable and verified successful export to server.

helpfulDetails: Export tested via USB and network

title: Natus NicoletOne EEG System - Patient Event Marker or Input Failure
description: Troubleshooting patient event marker button not registering, delayed inputs, or missing annotations during EEG recording.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while actively recording a critical study if event capture is required. Pause or transition to a backup system if necessary.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to press the patient event marker and observe system response. Check if event appears in EEG recording software and note delay, no response, or intermittent behavior.

steps.title: Check Event Marker Connection (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Ensure cable is fully seated in the correct input port. Disconnect and reconnect firmly. Inspect connector pins for damage or debris.

steps.title: Inspect Cable and Button Condition
steps.instructions: Check for frayed cable, cracked housing, or stuck button. Press button multiple times to feel for mechanical response.

steps.title: Test with Known-Good Event Marker (If Available)
steps.instructions: Swap the event marker device with a working one.

steps.title: Verify Software Input Configuration
steps.instructions: Confirm correct input channel is assigned in software. Ensure event marker feature is enabled. Restart acquisition software if needed.

steps.title: Restart the System (Basic Reset)
steps.instructions: Close acquisition software. Reboot computer and relaunch system.

steps.title: Check Input Port Functionality
steps.instructions: Test the same port with another compatible input device (if applicable).

CCR.Complaint: Patient event marker not registering during EEG study.
CCR.Cause: Failed event marker cable with intermittent internal break.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced event marker device and verified proper event logging.

title: Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor EEG System - Poor electrode impedance or unstable signal
description: Troubleshooting poor electrode impedance, noisy EEG tracing, unstable signal, or intermittent lead quality before assuming monitor failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Olympic Brainz Monitor is connected to a patient, do not disturb electrodes or cables without coordinating with clinical staff. Confirm the patient is stable and that monitoring can be safely interrupted if electrodes need to be adjusted or replaced. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is managed safely before troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: Poor signal troubleshooting often involves touching electrodes, lead wires, or patient-applied accessories.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is: High electrode impedance; Intermittent impedance readings; Unstable EEG signal; Excessive artifact or noise; One bad channel; Multiple bad channels; Total signal loss. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is identified before parts are swapped. Why it matters: A single bad channel usually points toward an electrode, lead, or connection issue. Multiple unstable channels may suggest setup, environment, cable, or monitor input concerns.

steps.title: Check Electrode Placement and Skin Contact
steps.instructions: Inspect each electrode site for poor contact, lifting, dried gel, loose adhesive, hair interference, sweat, or skin oils. Re-prep the electrode site or have clinical staff replace the affected electrode according to facility policy. Expected outcome: Impedance improves and the signal stabilizes after restoring proper electrode contact. If this resolves the issue, return the device to service and document the electrode contact issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Electrode Set and Lead Wires
steps.instructions: Check the electrode leads for bent pins, damaged insulation, loose snaps, broken wires, contamination, or signs of repeated pulling. Gently flex the lead wires while observing the signal if it is safe to do so and the patient is not dependent on uninterrupted monitoring. Expected outcome: A damaged or intermittent lead is identified, or the cable remains stable during handling. If the signal drops out when a lead is moved, replace the electrode set or lead assembly and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm Electrodes Are Properly Connected
steps.instructions: Verify that each electrode is fully seated in the correct input location and that the connector is not partially inserted. Check for reversed, crossed, or mismatched leads if the signal appears abnormal. Expected outcome: Connections are secure and channel assignment is correct. Why it matters: A partially seated electrode connection can create unstable impedance or noisy tracing without any monitor failure.

steps.title: Check the Patient Cable or Input Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the connection between the electrode lead set, patient cable, and monitor input. Disconnect and reconnect the cable firmly, checking for looseness, bent pins, debris, or poor strain relief. Expected outcome: The connector seats securely and signal quality improves or remains unchanged. If reseating the cable restores stable signal, document the loose connection and verify continued operation before returning to use.

steps.title: Swap Known-Good Accessories When Available
steps.instructions: Test with a known-good electrode set, patient cable, or input cable if available and appropriate. Do not swap accessories between patients unless infection control policy allows it and the accessory is properly cleaned or single-use items are replaced. Expected outcome: The issue follows the accessory or remains with the monitor. Why it matters: Most impedance and unstable signal complaints are caused by electrodes, lead wires, cable connections, or setup conditions rather than the monitor itself.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Electrical Noise
steps.instructions: Look for nearby sources of interference such as warming devices, beds, infusion pumps, power supplies, charging equipment, or other electrical devices close to the patient cable. Move unnecessary electrical equipment away from the EEG leads when clinically appropriate. Confirm the Olympic Brainz Monitor is connected to an appropriate outlet and avoid unnecessary extension cords or non-approved power strips. Expected outcome: Noise decreases when interference sources are removed or separated.

steps.title: Inspect the Monitor for External Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Check the monitor, input connector area, cable ports, and power supply for fluid contamination, cracked housing, damaged pins, unusual heat, odor, or signs of impact. Expected outcome: No physical damage or contamination is present. If damage, fluid intrusion, burning smell, or overheating is found, remove the monitor from service immediately.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the signal remains unstable after electrode and cable checks, restart the monitor only after confirming patient monitoring can be safely interrupted or transferred. Expected outcome: The monitor boots normally and signal quality can be reassessed. If the issue clears after restart, observe briefly to confirm the signal remains stable before returning the unit to use.

steps.title: Confirm the Final Status
steps.instructions: After corrections are made, verify: Electrode impedance is acceptable; EEG signal is stable; No channels are dropping out; Cable movement does not cause signal loss; No abnormal noise or artifact remains; Monitor remains powered and responsive. Expected outcome: The monitor produces a stable signal using verified electrodes and cables.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported high electrode impedance and unstable EEG signal on the Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor during patient setup.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found one electrode lead intermittently losing contact when the cable was moved, causing poor impedance and signal artifact.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the affected electrode lead set, reseated the patient cable, verified stable impedance and EEG signal, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Which channel or electrode showed poor impedance
Whether the issue affected one lead or multiple leads
Known-good cable or electrode set tested
Whether cable movement changed the signal
Nearby electrical equipment or interference sources
Any visible damage, fluid, heat, smell, or contamination

title: Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor EEG System - No waveform displayed after patient connection
description: Troubleshooting missing EEG waveform display after patient connection due to lead, electrode, cable, setup, or device input issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is clinically stable and that troubleshooting will not delay needed monitoring or treatment. If EEG monitoring is clinically required, notify clinical staff and arrange an alternate monitoring method or backup EEG system if available. Expected outcome: Patient care is not dependent on a monitor that is not displaying waveforms.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Verify whether the Olympic Brainz Monitor powers on normally and whether the display is functioning. Check if the issue is truly no waveform, or if the screen is blank, frozen, dimmed, paused, or displaying an error message. Expected outcome: The failure is confirmed as no waveform after patient connection, not a general power or display issue.

steps.title: Check That Monitoring Has Been Started
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is in an active monitoring or recording mode and has not been left in standby, paused, review, demo, or setup mode. Look for obvious screen indicators showing that acquisition is active. Expected outcome: The monitor is actively attempting to acquire EEG data. If starting or resuming monitoring restores the waveform, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Patient Cable Connection at the Monitor
steps.instructions: Inspect the patient cable connection to the Olympic Brainz Monitor. Make sure the connector is fully seated, correctly oriented, and not partially inserted. Gently check for looseness at the input connection without stressing the cable. Expected outcome: The patient cable is securely connected with no loose or intermittent connection. If reseating the cable restores the waveform, stop troubleshooting and document the loose connection.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Cable and Lead Set
steps.instructions: Examine the patient cable, electrode leads, and connectors for: Bent pins; Broken strain relief; Crushed or kinked cable sections; Torn insulation; Fluid contamination; Corrosion or residue inside connectors. Expected outcome: Cable and lead set appear intact and safe to use. If damage is found, remove the cable or lead set from service and replace it.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Electrode / Sensor Application
steps.instructions: Verify with clinical staff that the electrodes or sensors are applied correctly for the intended EEG monitoring setup. Check that electrodes are placed securely and have not lifted from the skin. Expected outcome: Electrodes are attached correctly and have good patient contact. Poor electrode contact can prevent usable EEG signal from appearing.

steps.title: Check Electrode Skin Contact and Prep
steps.instructions: Confirm that the skin was prepared according to clinical procedure and that electrode gel, adhesive, or sensor contact is adequate. Look for dry electrodes, poor adhesion, hair interference, sweat, lotion, or skin oils affecting contact. Expected outcome: Electrode contact is acceptable and not causing loss of signal. If improving electrode contact restores the waveform, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Lead-Off or Impedance Warnings
steps.instructions: Look for any lead-off, high impedance, disconnected electrode, or poor contact messages on the monitor. If available, review the impedance or signal quality indicators. Expected outcome: The monitor identifies whether the issue is related to poor electrode contact or disconnected leads. If high impedance or lead-off is present, correct the electrode or lead issue before suspecting the monitor.

steps.title: Verify All Required Leads Are Connected
steps.instructions: Confirm that the required electrodes, reference, ground, or common leads are connected as needed for the monitoring configuration. A missing reference or ground connection may prevent normal waveform display even if other electrodes are attached. Expected outcome: The complete patient connection path is present.

steps.title: Swap Known-Good Disposable Electrodes or Sensors
steps.instructions: If the issue persists, replace the electrodes or sensors with a known-good set. Avoid reusing questionable disposables if adhesion, gel quality, or packaging integrity is uncertain. Expected outcome: A defective or poorly contacting disposable is ruled out. If the waveform appears with new electrodes or sensors, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Swap the Patient Cable or Lead Set
steps.instructions: Test with a known-good compatible patient cable or lead set if available. Do not continue using a cable that causes intermittent signal loss when moved or flexed. Expected outcome: A cable or lead set fault is either confirmed or ruled out. If the waveform appears with a replacement cable, remove the failed cable from service.

steps.title: Check Display Scale, Filters, and Channel Visibility
steps.instructions: Confirm the waveform is not hidden due to screen layout, channel selection, gain, sensitivity, filter, or display configuration. Reset the view to a normal acquisition screen if appropriate. Expected outcome: Waveform channels are visible and scaled appropriately for clinical review.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor Safely
steps.instructions: If patient care allows and clinical staff approves, disconnect from active use and restart the Olympic Brainz Monitor. Reconnect the known-good cable and electrodes after restart. Expected outcome: Temporary software or acquisition lockup is cleared. If waveform display returns after restart, monitor for recurrence and document the behavior.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Interference Issues
steps.instructions: Look for strong electrical interference sources near the patient or monitor, including damaged power cords, nearby equipment, poorly grounded accessories, or excessive cable movement. Separate EEG leads from power cords when possible. Expected outcome: Signal acquisition is not being masked by avoidable interference.

steps.title: Verify the Issue Across More Than One Setup
steps.instructions: If available, test the monitor with a known-good patient simulator, test fixture, or known-good electrode/cable setup according to facility procedure. Expected outcome: The issue is isolated to either patient setup/accessories or the monitor itself. If no waveform appears with known-good accessories, suspect an internal acquisition or input issue.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor powered on but displayed no EEG waveform after patient connection.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found poor electrode contact and high impedance at the patient connection, preventing usable waveform acquisition.
CCR.Resolution: Electrodes were reapplied with proper skin contact, patient cable was reseated, waveform display returned, and monitor was left in service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the monitor powered on normally
Whether the screen was blank, frozen, or active with no waveform
Environmental factors or interference sources noted
Indicator lights or acquisition status shown

title: Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor EEG System - Battery Will Not Charge Or Rapidly Deplete
description: Troubleshooting charging failure, short battery runtime, AC power issues, or battery-related shutdowns on the Olympic Brainz Monitor.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Olympic Brainz Monitor is actively being used for patient EEG monitoring, do not troubleshoot power or charging issues while the patient is dependent on the device. Confirm clinical staff has moved monitoring to another available system or that monitoring can safely pause per clinical direction. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained safely before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Battery Problem
steps.instructions: Ask staff what was observed: Battery will not charge; Battery indicator does not increase; Monitor shuts down quickly when unplugged; Battery drains faster than expected; Device only works while connected to AC power; Charging indicator does not appear. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before parts or internal battery failure are assumed.

steps.title: Verify AC Power at the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Plug a known-good device or outlet tester into the same wall outlet. Confirm the outlet is live and not controlled by a wall switch, power strip, or tripped breaker. Expected outcome: AC power is available at the outlet. If the outlet is dead, use a known-good outlet and report the facility power issue as appropriate.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and External Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check the power cord and power adapter for: Loose plug connection; Bent connector; Damaged insulation; Cracked power brick; Strain relief damage; Missing or incorrect adapter. Reseat the power cord at the outlet, power supply, and monitor. Expected outcome: The external power path is secure and undamaged. If damage is found, remove the damaged accessory from use and replace it with an approved compatible power supply or cord.

steps.title: Check for Charging Indicators
steps.instructions: Connect the monitor to AC power and observe the display or charging indicator. Look for battery icon behavior, charging status, power LED status, or any warning message. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes AC power and shows charging activity. If no charging indication appears, continue checking the external power path before assuming battery failure.

steps.title: Test with a Known-Good Power Cord or Adapter if Available
steps.instructions: If the power cord or adapter is removable and a known-good compatible replacement is available, swap only the external accessory. Do not substitute an unverified power adapter with the wrong voltage, polarity, connector type, or current rating. Expected outcome: Charging resumes if the original cord or adapter was faulty. If charging resumes, document the accessory failure, replace the defective part, and stop.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Charging Time
steps.instructions: If the battery was fully depleted, leave the monitor connected to AC power long enough to recover before judging runtime. Confirm the device remains powered during charging and that the battery level increases over time. Expected outcome: Battery charge level rises while connected to AC power. If the battery level does not increase after a reasonable charging period, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Excessive Battery Drain Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify whether the monitor is being left on battery power longer than expected, stored unplugged, or used repeatedly without full recharge. Confirm staff is not mistaking normal battery discharge for a charging failure. Expected outcome: Use pattern supports proper charging and normal battery operation. If battery runtime remains unusually short after full charge, suspect battery degradation.

steps.title: Inspect the Monitor for Signs of Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Check the monitor and charging connector area for: Fluid ingress; Corrosion; Cracked housing; Loose charging jack; Burnt smell; Unusual heat; Intermittent power when the connector is moved. Expected outcome: No physical damage, overheating, or connector instability is present. If any of these are found, remove the monitor from service and send it for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Battery Runtime Check
steps.instructions: Fully charge the monitor, then operate it on battery under a safe non-patient test condition. Observe whether the monitor shuts down quickly, battery percentage drops rapidly, or power warnings appear sooner than expected. Expected outcome: Battery runtime is stable and appropriate for the device’s condition. If the battery rapidly depletes after a full charge, the battery is likely degraded or the charging circuit may not be maintaining charge properly.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor and Recheck Charging Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the monitor off, reconnect AC power, and power it back on if appropriate. Confirm whether the charging status changes after restart. Expected outcome: A temporary display or charging status issue clears. If the same charging problem returns, continue to escalation.

steps.title: Do Not Open the Device for Battery or Charging Circuit Repair During Floor Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Do not perform internal battery replacement, charging board inspection, or connector repair in the clinical area. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting remains limited to safe external checks and Clinical Engineering evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor would not hold a charge and shut down shortly after being unplugged from AC power.
CCR.Cause: Verified wall power and external power supply were functional, but the monitor battery depleted rapidly after a full charging attempt.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Olympic Brainz Monitor from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation for suspected battery or internal charging failure.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or power-related error messages
Accessories swapped, including power cord or power adapter
Power behavior on AC power and battery power
Environmental factors, including storage unplugged or repeated use without full recharge
Indicator lights, battery icon behavior, and charging indicator status
Final device status, including whether it was returned to service or labeled Out of Service

title: Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor EEG System - Touchscreen or user interface lockup
description: Troubleshooting touchscreen freeze, delayed response, or user interface lockup before removing the EEG monitor from service.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Olympic Brainz Monitor is being used on an active patient, do not attempt extended troubleshooting while monitoring is clinically needed. Notify clinical staff and confirm whether EEG monitoring must continue on another available monitor. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring is maintained without relying on a locked or unresponsive user interface. Why it matters: A frozen interface may prevent alarm review, trend review, event marking, or proper monitoring adjustments.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Interface Problem
steps.instructions: Observe the monitor and determine whether the issue is: Touchscreen does not respond; Screen image is frozen; Buttons respond slowly; System menus will not open; Cursor or touch input appears offset; Monitor is still displaying waveforms but controls are locked; Monitor is fully frozen with no visible updates. Expected outcome: The lockup behavior is confirmed and described clearly. If the touchscreen begins responding normally during this check, verify basic function with staff and return the monitor to service if no further issue is present.

steps.title: Check for Physical Contact or Obstruction on the Screen
steps.instructions: Inspect the touchscreen for anything causing constant contact, such as: Tape; Labels; Gel residue; Cleaning solution residue; Gloves or cloth resting on the screen; Cracked or damaged screen surface; Pressure from a cover or accessory. Clean the screen using facility-approved method and manufacturer-compatible cleaning products. Expected outcome: The touchscreen responds normally after removing contamination or pressure. If this resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Monitor Is Not in a Startup, Shutdown, or Processing State
steps.instructions: Check whether the system recently powered on, was ending a study, saving data, or attempting to load patient information. Allow the monitor a reasonable amount of time to complete the visible process before cycling power. Expected outcome: The interface resumes once processing is complete. Why it matters: Some apparent lockups may be delayed response during file handling or system startup rather than a true touchscreen failure.

steps.title: Check External Power Stability
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is connected to a known-good AC outlet and that the power supply is fully seated. Inspect the power cord and external power supply for: Loose wall connection; Loose monitor-side connection; Damaged cable jacket; Bent connector; Power brick indicator behavior, if present; Signs of overheating or burning smell. Test the outlet or move the monitor to a verified outlet if needed. Expected outcome: The monitor has stable power and the interface responds normally. If unstable power or a loose connection caused the lockup, correct the power issue, verify normal operation, and stop.

steps.title: Check for Connected Accessories Causing System Delay
steps.instructions: If safe to do so and the monitor is not actively needed for patient care, disconnect nonessential external accessories one at a time. Check items such as: USB storage device; External keyboard or mouse; Network cable; External display connection, if used; Peripheral cables connected to the monitor. After each removal, observe whether the touchscreen response returns. Expected outcome: A faulty or incompatible external accessory is identified, or accessories are ruled out. If a specific accessory causes the lockup, remove that accessory from use and document it.

steps.title: Check Network or Data Connection Behavior
steps.instructions: If the lockup occurs when opening patient files, saving studies, or accessing network functions, check whether the issue may be related to a slow or unavailable network connection. Confirm with IT or clinical staff whether there are known network issues in that area. Expected outcome: Network-related delay is separated from a true monitor touchscreen failure. Why it matters: A system may appear frozen while waiting on a failed network path or data transfer.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart When Safe
steps.instructions: If the user interface remains locked and the monitor is not actively needed for patient monitoring, perform a controlled power cycle according to facility policy. Power the monitor down normally if possible. If the interface will not allow normal shutdown, remove it from patient use first, then restart only when safe. Expected outcome: The monitor restarts successfully and touchscreen function returns. If the monitor operates normally after restart, perform a basic functional check before returning it to service.

steps.title: Verify Basic Function After Restart
steps.instructions: After reboot, check: Touchscreen response; Menu navigation; Patient/study screen access; Alarm or status message area; Date and time display; Electrode/input status display, if applicable; Whether the same lockup returns. Expected outcome: The user interface responds normally and no recurring lockup occurs. If the issue is resolved and cannot be reproduced, return the monitor to service only if clinical staff agrees it is functioning normally.

steps.title: Look for Recurring Lockup Pattern
steps.instructions: Ask staff when the issue occurs: Immediately after startup; During active monitoring; When saving or closing a study; When using a USB device; When connected to network; After cleaning; After screen contact or movement; After several hours of operation. Expected outcome: A pattern is identified that helps determine whether the cause is external, workflow-related, accessory-related, or likely internal.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Hardware or Software Fault
steps.instructions: Remove the monitor from service if any of the following are present: Lockup returns after restart; Touchscreen remains unresponsive; Display freezes repeatedly; System fails to boot normally; Screen is cracked or physically damaged; Monitor becomes unusually hot; Burning smell or visible damage is present; Error messages appear repeatedly; Patient monitoring cannot be reliably controlled. Expected outcome: An unreliable EEG monitor is not returned to patient care.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor touchscreen froze during EEG monitoring and menus would not respond.
CCR.Cause: Touchscreen lockup was confirmed; power connection, screen contamination, USB accessories, and restart behavior were checked with no external cause found.
CCR.Resolution: Monitor was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation due to recurring user interface lockup.

helpfulDetails: Whether the monitor was on a patient
Whether the issue returned after restart

title: Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor EEG System - Printer / Data Export Failure
description: Troubleshooting printer, report printing, USB, network, or data export failures on the Olympic Brainz Monitor.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the EEG monitoring session is not being interrupted for an active patient before troubleshooting printing or export functions. If the monitor is actively being used for patient monitoring, do not power cycle, disconnect accessories, or remove storage media unless clinical staff confirms it is safe. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring remains uninterrupted while non-urgent printer or export issues are assessed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Ask the user what specifically failed: Report would not print; Printer not detected; Export option unavailable; USB device not recognized; Export file missing or incomplete; Data transfer failed; Error message during print or export. Expected outcome: The complaint is narrowed to printer output, removable media, network transfer, or stored data access.

steps.title: Confirm the Patient Data Is Still Viewable
steps.instructions: Before attempting corrective actions, verify the required EEG/aEEG recording or report is still accessible on the monitor. Do not delete studies, clear storage, or reformat media during basic troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The needed patient recording is preserved before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Check Printer Power and Basic Status
steps.instructions: If an external printer is used, confirm it is powered on, has paper installed, has no paper jam, and shows no error condition. Check for printer indicators such as offline status, low paper, low toner/ink, or fault lights. Expected outcome: Printer is ready and not failing due to a basic supply or status issue. If this resolves the issue, document the printer condition and stop.

steps.title: Check Printer Cable or Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the printer connection between the Olympic Brainz Monitor and printer. Confirm USB, serial, or network cables are fully seated and not damaged, stretched, or loose. If available, reseat the cable and test with a known-good cable. Expected outcome: The printer connection is secure and communication can be reattempted. If printing resumes after reseating or replacing the cable, return the system to service if no other issues are present.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Printer Is Selected
steps.instructions: Check the monitor’s print or report output settings, if accessible. Confirm the selected printer or output destination matches the connected printer. Expected outcome: The monitor is not attempting to print to an unavailable or incorrect destination.

steps.title: Test Printer Function Outside the Monitor if Possible
steps.instructions: If the printer can be tested independently, print a self-test page or connect it to another approved workstation. Expected outcome: The printer itself is confirmed functional. If the printer fails independent testing, remove or replace the printer rather than assuming the EEG monitor has failed.

steps.title: Check USB Storage Media for Data Export
steps.instructions: If the issue involves USB export, confirm the USB device is properly inserted and recognized. Use facility-approved removable media only. Inspect the USB drive for physical damage, bent connector contacts, or signs of wear. Expected outcome: The USB device is physically intact and properly connected.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good USB Device
steps.instructions: Attempt export using a known-good, approved USB drive. Avoid using encrypted, damaged, unusually large, or unknown-format USB drives unless supported by facility policy and the device workflow. Expected outcome: Export succeeds with known-good media. If export works with another USB device, the original USB drive should be removed from use or reformatted/replaced according to IT and facility policy.

steps.title: Check Available Storage and File Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct patient study, report, time range, or export option was selected. Verify the export destination has enough available space. Expected outcome: The export is not failing because of an incorrect selection or lack of storage space.

steps.title: Look for Error Messages or Export Codes
steps.instructions: Record any displayed message exactly as shown. Note whether the failure occurs immediately, during file preparation, during transfer, or after the export appears complete. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is documented for repair, IT, or vendor support if escalation is needed.

steps.title: Check Network Connection if Export Uses Network Transfer
steps.instructions: If the Olympic Brainz Monitor exports through a network location or hospital system, verify the network cable is connected or wireless/network status is normal, depending on the installed configuration. Check whether other devices in the same area are having network issues. Expected outcome: A local network or hospital IT issue is ruled in or ruled out.

steps.title: Confirm Date, Time, and Patient Identification
steps.instructions: Check that the device date/time and patient/study information appear correct. Incorrect study selection or mismatched patient information can lead staff to believe an export failed when the file was saved under a different identifier. Expected outcome: The exported file can be matched to the correct patient record or study.

steps.title: Restart Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If the monitor is not actively being used for patient monitoring, perform a normal shutdown and restart. Do not force power removal unless the unit is locked up and facility procedure allows it. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication issues clear after a controlled restart. If printing or export works after restart, document the action and verify normal operation before returning to use.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check
steps.instructions: After corrective action, perform a test print or test export using non-patient or approved test data when possible. Confirm the file opens or the report prints correctly. Expected outcome: Printer or export function is verified before the system is returned for clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Natus Olympic Brainz Monitor would not export EEG recording data to USB after patient monitoring was completed.
CCR.Cause: Verified the original USB drive was not recognized by the monitor; export completed successfully using a known-good approved USB drive.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the faulty USB media, confirmed successful test export, and returned the Olympic Brainz Monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue was print, USB export, or network export
Patient data still viewable on monitor
Printer power and paper status checked
USB drive swapped with known-good media
Export file name or study identifier verified
